items.content_statement: Steady growth of New Zealand's population over the last two centuries
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional content about demographic trends - Type 1 knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Changes in population structure, including an ageing population
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about demographic patterns - factual content about population changes that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Declining death rates due to improved medical knowledge, healthcare access, and lifestyle changes
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about causal factors affecting death rates - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Division of New Zealand into 16 regions
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional content about geographic administrative divisions - Type 1 knowledge

items.content_statement: Migration is a factor affecting population size and structure.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual claim about how migration affects population that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Push factors such as war, disasters, and lack of opportunities
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual content about categories of push factors that cause population movement

items.content_statement: Pull factors such as work opportunities, culture, and education
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about migration factors - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Multicultural population results from migration by different groups.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/causal claim about how multicultural populations form - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Population density as a measure of people living in an area
items.classification_rationale: Definitional/conceptual knowledge - what population density is as a measure

items.content_statement: Low population density in New Zealand, especially on the South Island
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional content about demographic characteristics - Type 1 knowledge

items.content_statement: Movement of Earth's plates into, away from, or past each other
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge describing factual content about tectonic plate movement - Type 1 factual knowledge

items.content_statement: Earthquakes are caused by pressure building and releasing along plate boundaries.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claim about how earthquakes work that learners must understand as factual content

items.content_statement: Ground shaking can lead to building damage, collapses, landslides, and rockfalls
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claim about how ground shaking affects physical structures and terrain

items.content_statement: Earthquake risk known in some areas, but timing cannot be predicted
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about earthquake predictability - factual content about what can and cannot be known scientifically

items.content_statement: Earthquake preparation through building design and civil defence planning
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how earthquake preparation works through specific methods - factual/conceptual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Earthquake planning requiring funding and organisation
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the requirements for earthquake planning - factual content about what earthquake planning needs

items.content_statement: Earthquakes can cause tsunamis.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - a factual causal claim about how the world works that learners must hold as factual content

items.content_statement: Volcano formation from magma reaching the surface as lava.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about a geological process - factual content that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: Variation in eruption strength and lava viscosity
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about volcanic characteristics - factual content about how eruptions vary

items.content_statement: Less explosive eruptions producing flowing lava
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about volcanic eruption characteristics - factual content about how certain eruptions behave

items.content_statement: More explosive eruptions releasing ash and volcanic bombs
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about volcanic eruption characteristics - factual content about what explosive eruptions produce

items.content_statement: Volcano prediction through monitoring tremors, land distortion, and temperature changes.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how volcano prediction works - describes a causal/procedural relationship that learners must understand as factual content

items.content_statement: Eruption impacts include evacuation, damage to farmland and buildings, injury, or death
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about factual consequences of volcanic eruptions - hard prerequisite sequence applies as this builds on understanding what eruptions are

items.content_statement: Financial aid and humanitarian assistance provided by individuals and communities
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about types of support responses to tectonic hazards - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Urbanisation is the process of more people living in cities
items.classification_rationale: Definitional propositional knowledge - a factual claim about what urbanisation means

items.content_statement: Growth of megacities as a result of urbanisation
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about a causal relationship - urbanisation causes megacity growth. This is factual content to be understood.

items.content_statement: Urban living has been the norm in high-income countries since the mid-19th century
items.classification_rationale: Factual/historical claim about when urbanisation became dominant in high-income countries - propositional knowledge

items.content_statement: Rapid urban growth in low- and middle-income countries
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about a demographic/geographic phenomenon - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Economic, social, and environmental problems caused by rapid urban growth
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about causal relationships - factual content about what problems result from rapid urbanization

items.content_statement: Informal settlements in low- and middle-income countries with specific challenges
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about the existence and characteristics of informal settlements - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: City growth during the 19th and 20th centuries
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical facts and patterns that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Influence of European settler culture on city development
items.classification_rationale: This describes a conceptual relationship (how European settler culture influenced urban development) that learners must understand - it's propositional knowledge about historical causation

items.content_statement: Impact of urbanisation and migration on population diversity
items.classification_rationale: This describes propositional knowledge about causal relationships - how urbanisation and migration affect population diversity. This is conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation of the mechanisms and effects.

items.content_statement: Mid-20th century Māori migration to New Zealand cities occurred at an unprecedented pace and scale, disrupting the whakapapa of te reo Māori and tikanga and depopulating papa kāinga
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - historical fact about timing, scale, and consequences of Māori urbanisation

items.content_statement: New approaches to being Māori and retaining iwi values and practices were created and debated during urbanisation
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about historical developments - factual content about what happened

items.content_statement: Movements to reassert Māori language, culture, and identity arose throughout the country, including cultural groups, political groups, tribal affiliate groups in cities, urban pan-tribal marae, and educational movements
items.classification_rationale: Factual knowledge about specific historical movements and their types - propositional content to be learned

items.content_statement: how natural increase in population can be calculated
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with fixed steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: similarities and differences of different ways of showing population density (maps, pie charts, 3D models, interactive graphics)
items.classification_rationale: analytical comparison across multiple representation methods - Type 2

items.content_statement: how diagrams can be used to demonstrate the impact of plate movement on the creation of hazards
items.classification_rationale: analytical skill for demonstrating causal relationships - Type 2

items.content_statement: how tectonic processes have created land and hazards
items.classification_rationale: causal understanding of geological processes - Type 1

items.content_statement: how buildings can be designed to withstand earthquakes
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of engineering principles - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret data on the relative magnitude of earthquakes and the associated hazards
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill for data interpretation with clear criteria - Type 1

items.content_statement: how the features of explosive and effusive volcanic eruptions can be categorised
items.classification_rationale: analytical categorisation skill requiring reasoning about multiple features - Type 2

items.content_statement: why earthquakes and volcanoes are managed in different ways
items.classification_rationale: analytical reasoning about management approaches across different contexts - Type 2

items.content_statement: how to interpret a range of data to analyse how a named area was affected by tectonic hazards
items.classification_rationale: analytical interpretation skill across multiple data sources - Type 2

items.content_statement: how trends can be analysed using visual geographic data displaying the percentage of urban population
items.classification_rationale: analytical skill for trend analysis requiring reasoning about patterns - Type 2

items.content_statement: how the percentage change in the urban population can be calculated by analysing population data
items.classification_rationale: procedural calculation skill with fixed steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how aerial photographs and historic maps can be used to chart the way that cities have grown and changed over time
items.classification_rationale: analytical skill using multiple evidence sources to trace change - Type 2

items.content_statement: how photographs, aerial photographs, and first-hand testimony can be used to identify the challenges of living in informal settlements
items.classification_rationale: analytical skill using multiple evidence sources for identification and analysis - Type 2

items.content_statement: the characteristics of New Zealand's cities
items.classification_rationale: factual knowledge about specific geographic features - Type 1

items.content_statement: The Earth's crust is made of large plates, moving slowly.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual claim about how the Earth's crust is structured and behaves

items.content_statement: Increasing temperature with depth inside the Earth
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about Earth's thermal structure - Type 1 knowledge that can be assessed through knowledge test

items.content_statement: Mountain formation from plate collisions, including the creation of the Southern Alps 25 million years ago
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about geological processes and specific historical formation - factual content that must be understood

items.content_statement: Evidence of plate movement in mountain ranges, species distribution, and coastline patterns
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about factual evidence that can be learned and assessed through knowledge checks

items.content_statement: Sedimentary rock formed from compressed particles of other rocks (e.g. limestone).
items.classification_rationale: Definitional/factual content about how sedimentary rock forms - propositional knowledge that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Igneous rock formed from cooled lava or magma (e.g. pumice, granite)
items.classification_rationale: Definitional/factual content about rock formation - propositional knowledge that must be understood

items.content_statement: Metamorphic rock formed from heat and pressure on other rocks (e.g. pounamu).
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how metamorphic rocks form - factual content that must be understood

items.content_statement: Transformation of rock through the rock cycle
items.classification_rationale: Describes a geological process concept that builds on prerequisite understanding of rock types and geological processes in a hierarchical sequence

items.content_statement: Influence of rock type on landscape formation through different erosion rates
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about causal relationships in geological processes - how rock type affects landscape formation through erosion mechanisms

items.content_statement: Erosion by rivers, ice, and waves shaping the land
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about how natural processes shape landforms - factual content about geological processes

items.content_statement: Zealandia is a mostly submerged continent.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content - a definitional claim about a geographical feature that learners must hold as knowledge

items.content_statement: Formation of New Zealand from tectonic uplift 23 million years ago
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional content about geological history - hard prerequisite sequence applies

items.content_statement: Creation of mountains, volcanoes, and earthquakes by the Pacific Plate and Australian Plate
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about geological processes - factual content about how tectonic plates create landforms

items.content_statement: Geological processes that created the landscape
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about factual content - geological processes and their role in landscape formation

items.content_statement: Rock types and geological features
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual content about rock types and geological features that learners must hold

items.content_statement: New Zealand has large areas of very rare temperate rainforest
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional content about geographic distribution - Type 1 knowledge

items.content_statement: Temperate rainforest grows because westerly wind picks up moist air from across the ocean. The air rises over the mountains, cools, and turns into rains. This combines with mild temperatures to create good growing conditions.
items.classification_rationale: Hard prerequisite sequence - understanding the causal mechanism of temperate rainforest formation requires understanding atmospheric processes, orographic precipitation, and climate conditions in a fixed sequence

items.content_statement: The location of the world's major rainforest areas, including those in South America, West Africa, and South Asia.
items.classification_rationale: Factual/propositional knowledge about geographic locations - no prerequisite sequence required, assessable through knowledge test

items.content_statement: Biomes are global-scale ecosystems
items.classification_rationale: definitional claim - propositional knowledge about what biomes are

items.content_statement: Biomes have shared characteristics wherever they are in the world
items.classification_rationale: factual claim - propositional knowledge about biome properties

items.content_statement: Tropical rainforest biomes are found either side of the equator, where there are high levels of sunlight and rainfall, allowing rapid tree growth.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about geographic distribution and environmental conditions - factual content with causal relationships that learners must hold

items.content_statement: Rainforests have high levels of biodiversity
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content - a definitional claim about rainforest characteristics that learners must hold as knowledge

items.content_statement: Trees grow towards the sunlight. This creates distinct layers: emergent, canopy, under canopy, and the forest floor.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about forest structure - factual content about how tree growth creates forest layers

items.content_statement: Plants adapt to rainforest conditions.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about biological adaptation - a conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation of how and why plants develop specific adaptations for rainforest environments

items.content_statement: Competition for light means that plants try to grow as tall as they can, as quickly as they can, or they climb up other plants or grow directly on their branches.
items.classification_rationale: This is a propositional claim about how ecological competition works - a causal relationship between light competition and plant growth strategies. It's factual/conceptual content to be understood, not a skill to deploy or orientation to sustain.

items.content_statement: The soil in the rainforest has few nutrients, so trees have shallow roots to get nutrients from plant material as soon as it decomposes.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claim about how rainforest ecosystem structure relates to nutrient cycling

items.content_statement: Many people make their lives in rainforests. Some of these are Indigenous peoples who have maintained their traditional way of life.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about who lives in rainforests - factual knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Deforestation leads to large areas of rainforest being cleared and threatens ecosystems and the way of life of Indigenous peoples.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - causal claim about how deforestation affects ecosystems and Indigenous peoples

items.content_statement: Most deforestation takes place due to settlements, to raise cattle, or to grow crops such as palm, rubber, or coffee.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about causes of deforestation - factual knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Deforestation can also change the local and global climate.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional causal claim about how deforestation affects climate - factual/conceptual content to be understood

items.content_statement: People interact with and use places, resources, and environments for personal, social, cultural, and economic reasons.
items.classification_rationale: This is a propositional claim about how the world works - a conceptual understanding about human-environment interactions that learners must internalize. It describes a transferable concept rather than a hard prerequisite sequence or sustained orientation.

items.content_statement: Changes people make to places can have long-term environmental impacts on those places, the people who live there, and global processes.
items.classification_rationale: This is a propositional/causal claim about how human actions affect environmental systems - conceptual knowledge to be understood rather than a skill to deploy or orientation to sustain

items.content_statement: Values and connection to place affect their sense of responsibility for environmental sustainability.
items.classification_rationale: This is a propositional claim about how the world works - a causal relationship between values/place connection and environmental responsibility. It describes factual/conceptual content the learner must understand, not a skill to deploy or orientation to sustain.

items.content_statement: Past climate changes, including glacial and inter-glacial periods
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about factual content - historical climate patterns that learners must hold as foundational knowledge

items.content_statement: Greenhouse effect as the atmosphere storing and reflecting heat from the Sun
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge describing how a natural process works - conceptual understanding that can be assessed through explanation

items.content_statement: Current climate warming caused by human actions changing the atmosphere
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/causal claim about how the world works - factual content the learner must hold

items.content_statement: Greenhouse gases trapping heat and causing global warming
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/causal claim about how the world works - describes a scientific mechanism that learners must understand

items.content_statement: Land degradation in dry and how areas means land is less productive
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual claim about causal relationship between land degradation and productivity

items.content_statement: Disruption of weather patterns, with heavy rain in some places and less rain in others
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about climate pattern effects - factual content about how climate change manifests in weather disruption

items.content_statement: Melting glaciers and warming oceans leading to sea-level rise
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge describing a causal relationship in climate science - factual content about how specific processes lead to sea-level rise

items.content_statement: Rising temperatures reducing snowfall in alpine areas
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/causal claim about how the world works - describes a factual relationship between temperature and snowfall patterns

items.content_statement: Rising sea levels threatening homes and Māori coastal marae
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about a factual phenomenon - describes a causal relationship between sea level rise and threats to specific locations

items.content_statement: Increasing frequency and strength of cyclones
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about climate patterns that learners must hold as knowledge

items.content_statement: Risk of submersion and climate displacement in Pacific nations such as Tuvalu
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about specific climate risks and their geographic impacts - factual content to be understood

items.content_statement: Mitigation through global efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about climate mitigation strategies - factual content about how mitigation works

items.content_statement: Adaptation through preparation for climate change impacts
items.classification_rationale: Describes a sustained default orientation toward preparation and adaptation that operates across contexts, not an occasion-triggered skill

items.content_statement: how diagrams demonstrate the formation of landforms
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how tectonics will continue to cause the landscape to change in the future
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of causal processes - Type 1

items.content_statement: observations about different types of rocks can be collected and recorded
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret aerial photographs and maps to identify different landscapes
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret geological maps
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how the erosion of rock shapes the land
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of causal processes - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret maps of biomes to identify patterns in the location of tropical rainforests
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: diagrams can be used to identify the structure of the rainforest
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how plant adaptations match the environmental conditions
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of causal relationships - Type 1

items.content_statement: how secondary data on rainforest clearance can be collected and analysed using aerial photographs and simple graphs
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: the connection between economic development and deforestation
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of causal relationships - Type 1

items.content_statement: how deforestation changes the environment
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of causal processes - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret line graphs showing global temperature changes at a range of time scales
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: how to interpret graphs to identify the correlation between rising greenhouse gas concentration and rising global temperatures
items.classification_rationale: procedural skill with clear steps - Type 1

items.content_statement: a range of data can be used to evaluate attempts to manage climate change through both mitigation and adaptation
items.classification_rationale: analytical capability requiring reasoning across multiple frameworks - Type 2

items.content_statement: the impacts of climate change on people and the environment and how impacts can be categorised as economic, social, and environmental
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding with clear categorisation framework - Type 1

items.content_statement: Environmental regions (biomes) are defined by climate, vegetation, and landforms such as deserts, rainforests, tundra, and grasslands.
items.classification_rationale: Definitional propositional knowledge - states factual content about how biomes are classified and what defines them

items.content_statement: Tropical climate caused by the equator running through Southeast Asia, with high temperatures and heavy rainfall.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual claim about geographic causation that learners must hold as foundational content

items.content_statement: Tropical rainforests as the dominant ecosystem
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about ecosystem classification - factual content that can be assessed through knowledge check

items.content_statement: Mountains and frequent volcanic eruptions and earthquakes caused by tectonic processes
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about geological features and their causal relationship to tectonic processes - factual content with clear correct/incorrect answers

items.content_statement: A region is an area with shared characteristics and internal differences.
items.classification_rationale: Definitional claim about what a region is - propositional knowledge that must be understood

items.content_statement: Southeast Asia is made up of 11 countries: Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar (Burma), Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Timor-Leste (East Timor), and Vietnam.
items.classification_rationale: Factual propositional knowledge - specific geographic information that can be memorized and recalled

items.content_statement: Cultural diversity, with over 1,200 languages spoken.
items.classification_rationale: Factual propositional content about Southeast Asia's linguistic diversity - Type 1 knowledge to be held

items.content_statement: Mixed levels of economic development and activity across the region.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual claim about economic conditions in Southeast Asia that learners must hold as factual content

items.content_statement: Agriculture and manufacturing as key parts of the economy.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge about economic sectors - factual content about what constitutes key parts of Southeast Asian economies

items.content_statement: Goods produced in Southeast Asia sold in other countries such as New Zealand
items.classification_rationale: Propositional/factual content about trade relationships - hard prerequisite sequence applies as geographic and economic concepts build on each other

items.content_statement: Location of the country.
items.classification_rationale: Propositional knowledge - factual content about geographic location that learners must hold

items.content_statement: how longitude and latitude can be used to describe a location
items.classification_rationale: propositional knowledge about coordinate systems - hard prerequisite sequence exists

items.content_statement: how maps can show the way biomes are distributed
items.classification_rationale: conceptual understanding of map representation - builds on coordinate knowledge

items.content_statement: why it is hotter at the equator than the poles
items.classification_rationale: causal understanding of climate patterns - propositional knowledge with clear explanation

items.content_statement: how a range of geographical information (photographs, maps, simple graphs) can be interpreted to describe the culture of one country
items.classification_rationale: analytical skill deployed when interpreting geographical data - multiple valid interpretive approaches
